thai: บะหมี่น้ำมันกระเทียมไก่

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai garlic oil noodles with chicken; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002700) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific Thai garlic oil noodle code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to Thai garlic oil noodles with chicken at a useful level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of garlic oil noodles with chicken is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of cooked egg noodles tossed in garlic-infused oil with sliced or shredded chicken and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles, egg, cooked and Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, stir-fried — composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) with FAO/INFOODS SEA adjustment for Thai condiment contribution
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai garlic oil noodles with chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked egg noodles (FDC ID 168930) and stir-fried chicken breast (FDC ID 171477), combined in approximate proportions of 55% noodles and 30% chicken by weight, with 15% contribution from garlic oil, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and fish sauce. Sodium is elevated to reflect condiment contribution. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are weighted composites. All values are per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: Noodles, egg, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to garlic oil noodle dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai garlic oil noodles with chicken. The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 40 (low) for boiled egg noodles. Garlic oil, chicken protein, and fat content in this dish are expected to further attenuate the glycemic response relative to plain boiled noodles. A GI of 40 is therefore used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 71.75) / 100 ≈ 28.7, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 20.5 × 3.5 = 71.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, soy sauce, and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 350 g serving provides approximately 1820 mg sodium — approaching the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from egg noodles with low GI attenuation by fat and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of egg noodles (GI ≈ 40) and the co-presence of protein and fat moderate the postprandial glucose response, but the total carbohydrate load per serving (~72 g) warrants portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content, partially offset by lean chicken protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content from multiple condiments poses a cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should opt for smaller portions and request reduced-sodium preparation, while the lean chicken component provides a favourable protein-to-saturated-fat ratio.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Oil Noodles with Chicken (Thai: บะหมี่น้ำมันกระเทียมไก่, romanized: ba-mi nam-man kra-thiam kai) is a Thai noodle dish consisting of cooked egg noodles tossed in fragrant garlic-infused oil, served with sliced or shredded chicken and seasoned with oyster sauce, soy sauce, fish sauce, and white pepper. The dish is typically finished with spring onions and fried garlic for aroma. The texture is silky and slightly chewy from the egg noodles, with a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile dominated by roasted garlic. It is a popular street-food and restaurant staple in Thailand and is widely available in Thai restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as The Upper House at Funan. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 648 kcal, 40 g protein, 24 g fat, and 72 g carbohydrate. The egg noodle base has a low glycemic index (GI ≈ 40), which moderates postprandial glucose response, though the total carbohydrate load per serving is substantial. Sodium content is high due to the combination of fish sauce, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, making this dish a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains gluten (egg noodles), soy, and shellfish-derived ingredients (oyster sauce) and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the chicken and fish sauce components.
common_user_queries: Is garlic noodles with chicken healthy?
How many calories in Thai garlic noodles?
Is garlic oil noodles high in sodium?
Thai noodle dish with garlic and chicken

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai garlic oil noodles with chicken was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked egg noodles and stir-fried chicken, combined in proportions representative of the dish, with condiment sodium adjustment. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled egg noodles. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked egg noodles FDC ID 168930; stir-fried chicken breast FDC ID 171477) in approximate proportions of 55% noodles, 30% chicken, 15% garlic oil and condiments by weight. No exact approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. Sodium_mg (520 mg per 100 g) reflects elevated condiment contribution from fish sauce, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; this is a key clinical concern. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero as egg noodles and chicken contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the source database. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the source database. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data used. 7. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled egg noodles; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 8. Halal status is marked false because fish sauce and oyster sauce sourcing cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without restaurant-level verification. 9. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record; however, this dish is a mixed noodle-and-protein dish and could also be classified under 'Mixed'. The input classification is retained as the chicken component is the primary protein anchor. 10. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant plate; actual restaurant portion may vary.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from 'Garlic Oil Noodles Chicken' to canonical 'Garlic Oil Noodles with Chicken'.

Constructed composite nutrient estimates from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy with condiment sodium adjustment.
Applied analogous GI estimate of 40 from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled egg noodles.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency; all aliases_by_language strings present verbatim in flat aliases list.
Set ontology match_type values to only allowed values: 'closest_parent' or 'none'.
